---
title: "Multivariate Methods"
subtitle: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting --- Lecture 5"
author: "Department of Economics"
institute: "University of Nebraska at Omaha"
date: "Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, emory-clean.scss]
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.05
    logo: ""
    footer: "BSAD 8310 | University of Nebraska at Omaha | Spring 2026"
    embed-resources: true
bibliography: ../Bibliography_base.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/chicago-author-date
---

## Lecture 5: Outline

1. Beyond Univariate Forecasting
2. Vector Autoregression (VAR)
3. ARIMAX Models
4. Cointegration and Error Correction
5. Key Takeaways and Roadmap

---

# Beyond Univariate Forecasting

Sometimes other series carry information about $y$'s future that $y$'s own past cannot provide.

---

## The Univariate Limitation

All models in Lectures 1--4 use only $y_t$'s own history:
$$\hat{y}_{T+h|T} = f(y_T, y_{T-1}, \ldots)$$

**But many series are linked:**

- Consumer sentiment today $\to$ retail spending next month
- Interest rates today $\to$ housing starts in 3--6 months
- Unemployment $\to$ consumer credit defaults with a lag

::: {.keybox}
If series $x_t$ contains information about $y_{t+h}$ not already in $y_t, y_{t-1}, \ldots$, then using $x_t$ can reduce forecast error. **Granger causality** formalizes this idea.
:::

[*Socratic: can using $x_t$ ever hurt forecast accuracy? When?*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## The Multivariate Toolkit

::: {.smaller}

| **Model** | **Role of $x_t$** | **Use case** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **VAR**($p$) | Symmetric: $y$ and $x$ forecast each other | Macro, finance; unknown direction |
| **ARIMAX** | Exogenous: $x_t$ drives $y_t$, not vice versa | Clear causal direction |
| **ECM** | Error-correction: long-run equilibrium | Cointegrated I(1) pairs |

:::

**Supporting tools:**

- **Granger causality test:** does $x_t$ help predict $y_t$?
- **Cross-correlation function (CCF):** at which lag does $x_t$ predict $y_{t+k}$?
- **Cointegration test:** do two I(1) series share a long-run trend?

---

# Vector Autoregression (VAR)

A VAR treats all variables symmetrically: every variable can predict every other.

---

## The VAR(1) Model

For two stationary series $y_{1t}$ and $y_{2t}$, the VAR(1) is:
$$\begin{cases}
  y_{1t} = c_1 + a_{11}\,y_{1,t-1} + a_{12}\,y_{2,t-1} + \varepsilon_{1t}\\[4pt]
  y_{2t} = c_2 + a_{21}\,y_{1,t-1} + a_{22}\,y_{2,t-1} + \varepsilon_{2t}
\end{cases}$$

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
VAR($p$) Model
:::
::: {.smaller}
$$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1\mathbf{y}_{t-1} + \cdots + \mathbf{A}_p\mathbf{y}_{t-p} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t, \qquad \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\,\boldsymbol{\Sigma})$$

$\mathbf{y}_t$ is $k\!\times\!1$; each $\mathbf{A}_i$ is $k\!\times\!k$; $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is the $k\!\times\!k$ error covariance matrix. [@Sims1980]
:::
:::

**Key feature:** $a_{12}$ captures the effect of $y_{2,t-1}$ on $y_{1t}$ --- the cross-variable coefficient that univariate AR misses.

---

## Estimation and Order Selection

**Estimation:** each equation is estimated separately by OLS (equivalent to GLS since all equations share the same regressors).

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Order selection:**

- Fit VAR($p$) for $p = 0, 1, \ldots, p_{\max}$
- Select $p$ minimizing AIC or BIC:
$$\mathrm{BIC} = -2\hat{\ell} + \kappa\ln T, \quad \kappa = k^2p+k \text{ parameters}$$
- BIC preferred (penalizes overparameterization)
- Use *same* $p$ for all equations
:::

::: {.column width="46%"}
::: {.warningbox}
::: {.smaller}
**Parameter explosion:** VAR($p$) with $k$ variables has $k^2 p + k$ parameters total.

$k=5$, $p=4$: $5^2\times4+5=\mathbf{105}$ parameters!

Keep $k$ small ($\leq 4$) or use LASSO-VAR for large systems.
:::
:::
:::

::::

---

## Granger Causality

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Granger Causality
:::
::: {.smaller}
$x_t$ **Granger-causes** $y_t$ if past values of $x_t$ help predict $y_t$ beyond what $y_t$'s own past can explain. [@Granger1969]
:::
:::

**Test procedure (in a VAR):**

1. Fit full VAR (with $x$-lags) and restricted VAR (without $x$-lags)
2. F-test: $H_0$: all $a_{12,\ell} = 0$ ($\ell = 1,\ldots,p$; no Granger causality)

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}
*Lab 5 example:* Does consumer sentiment (UMCSENT) Granger-cause retail sales (RSXFS)? $H_0$: all sentiment lags $= 0$ in the retail equation.
:::

::: {.column width="36%"}
::: {.warningbox}
::: {.smaller}
Granger causality $\neq$ structural causality: tests *predictive content*, not mechanism.
:::
:::
:::

::::

---

## Impulse Response Functions (IRF)

**Question:** what is the dynamic effect of a one-unit shock to $y_{it}$ on $y_{jt}$ over future horizons $h = 0,1,\ldots$?

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
IRF
:::
::: {.smaller}
The **impulse response** at horizon $h$:
$$\mathrm{IRF}_{ji}(h) = \frac{\partial y_{j,T+h}}{\partial \varepsilon_{i,T}}$$
Trace the propagation of a shock through the VAR system.
:::
:::

**Orthogonalization:** Cholesky decomposition of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ to identify structural shocks; *ordering matters* --- place the most exogenous variable first.
:::

::: {.column width="47%"}
**IRF: sentiment $\to$ retail (simulated):**

```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 300 170" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:170px;">
  <!-- Dashed zero reference line (drawn first so axes render on top) -->
  <line x1="35" y1="140" x2="285" y2="140" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="5,4"/>
  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="35" y1="140" x2="285" y2="140" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="35" y1="140" x2="35"  y2="10"  stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- Y tick labels -->
  <text x="29" y="143" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end">0</text>
  <text x="29" y="83"  font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end">0.5</text>
  <text x="29" y="25"  font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end">1</text>
  <!-- X axis label -->
  <text x="290" y="157" font-size="9" fill="#525252" font-style="italic">h</text>
  <!-- IRF bars: rho^h for rho=0.85; h=0..10; x spacing = 22px; y scale: 0->140, 1->20; range=120px -->
  <!-- h=0: 1.000 -> height=120 -> y=20 -->
  <line x1="58"  y1="20"  x2="58"  y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="58"  cy="20"  r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- h=1: 0.850 -> height=102 -> y=38 -->
  <line x1="80"  y1="38"  x2="80"  y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="80"  cy="38"  r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- h=2: 0.723 -> height=87 -> y=53 -->
  <line x1="102" y1="53"  x2="102" y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="102" cy="53"  r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- h=3: 0.614 -> height=74 -> y=66 -->
  <line x1="124" y1="66"  x2="124" y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="124" cy="66"  r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- h=4: 0.522 -> height=63 -> y=77 -->
  <line x1="146" y1="77"  x2="146" y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="146" cy="77"  r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- h=5: 0.444 -> height=53 -> y=87 -->
  <line x1="168" y1="87"  x2="168" y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="168" cy="87"  r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- h=6: 0.377 -> height=45 -> y=95 -->
  <line x1="190" y1="95"  x2="190" y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="190" cy="95"  r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- h=7: 0.321 -> height=39 -> y=101 -->
  <line x1="212" y1="101" x2="212" y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="212" cy="101" r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- h=8: 0.272 -> height=33 -> y=107 -->
  <line x1="234" y1="107" x2="234" y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="234" cy="107" r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- h=9: 0.232 -> height=28 -> y=112 -->
  <line x1="256" y1="112" x2="256" y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="256" cy="112" r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- h=10: 0.197 -> height=24 -> y=116 -->
  <line x1="278" y1="116" x2="278" y2="140" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="6"/>
  <circle cx="278" cy="116" r="4" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- X tick labels -->
  <text x="58"  y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">0</text>
  <text x="80"  y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">1</text>
  <text x="102" y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">2</text>
  <text x="124" y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">3</text>
  <text x="146" y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">4</text>
  <text x="168" y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">5</text>
  <text x="190" y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">6</text>
  <text x="212" y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">7</text>
  <text x="234" y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">8</text>
  <text x="256" y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">9</text>
  <text x="278" y="154" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">10</text>
</svg>
```

[*Effect decays geometrically ($\rho^h$, here $\rho=0.85$); fades after $\approx 6$ periods.*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

## Forecasting with VAR

**1-step ahead:**
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{T+1|T} = \hat{\mathbf{c}} + \hat{\mathbf{A}}_1\mathbf{y}_T + \cdots + \hat{\mathbf{A}}_p\mathbf{y}_{T-p+1}$$

**Multi-step (recursive substitution):**
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{T+h|T} = \hat{\mathbf{c}} + \hat{\mathbf{A}}_1\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{T+h-1|T} + \cdots + \hat{\mathbf{A}}_p\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{T+h-p|T}$$

using $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{T+k|T} = \mathbf{y}_{T+k}$ for $k \leq 0$.

::: {.keybox}
VAR forecasts are particularly useful at short horizons and when cross-variable dynamics are strong. At long horizons the VAR reverts toward its unconditional mean, matching the theoretical property of stationary systems.
:::

[*Socratic: if $a_{12} = a_{21} = 0$ in a VAR(1), what model does each equation reduce to?*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# ARIMAX Models

When the causal direction is clear, augment ARIMA with an exogenous predictor.

---

## Regression with ARIMA Errors

Standard OLS forecasting (Lecture 2) assumes independent errors. In practice, residuals are often autocorrelated.

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
ARIMAX / Regression with ARIMA Errors
:::
::: {.smaller}
$$y_t = \underbrace{\mathbf{x}_t'\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{regression}} + \underbrace{\eta_t}_{\text{ARIMA error}}, \qquad \eta_t \sim \mathrm{ARIMA}(p,d,q)$$

The error $\eta_t$ captures the autocorrelation *not* explained by the regressors $\mathbf{x}_t$.
:::
:::

**Why not use OLS?**

- OLS is unbiased but *inefficient* (ignores error covariance)
- Standard errors are wrong $\Rightarrow$ invalid $t$-tests and prediction intervals (PIs)
- ARIMAX/SARIMAX jointly estimates $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and the ARIMA parameters $(\phi,\theta)$ by MLE $\Rightarrow$ correct inference

---

## Distributed Lag Effects

A **contemporaneous** regressor captures only the same-period effect:
$$y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_t + \eta_t$$

A **distributed lag** (DL) model captures delayed effects:
$$y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_t + \beta_2 x_{t-1} + \cdots + \beta_{L+1} x_{t-L} + \eta_t$$

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="56%"}
**Example:** advertising spend $x_t$ affects sales $y_t$ with a lagged carryover effect:

- $\beta_1$: immediate effect (same month)
- $\beta_2$: carryover from last month's ads
- Diminishing returns: $|\beta_1| > |\beta_2| > |\beta_3|$
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.keybox}
::: {.smaller}
**Cross-correlation function (CCF):** $r_k = \mathrm{Corr}(x_t,\, y_{t+k})$. Bars outside ${\pm}1.96/\!\sqrt{T}$ indicate significant lag $k$. Select $L$ at the largest significant $k$; refine by AIC/BIC.
:::
:::
:::

::::

---

## ARIMAX: Practical Implementation

**Differencing rule:** if both $y_t \sim I(1)$ and $x_t \sim I(1)$, difference *both* before fitting (unless cointegrated --- see below).

::: {.keybox}
**Python:** `statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax.SARIMAX` accepts an `exog` argument for exogenous regressors.

`SARIMAX(y, exog=X, order=(p,d,q),`
`seasonal_order=(P,D,Q,m)).fit()`
:::

**Model selection workflow:**

1. Unit-root test $y_t$ and $x_t$ separately $\to$ choose $d$
2. Plot CCF of $(\Delta^d y_t)$ vs.\ $(\Delta^d x_t)$ $\to$ choose lag $L$
3. Identify ARIMA$(p,d,q)$ for residuals via ACF/PACF
4. Select $(p,q)$ by AIC; check residuals with Ljung-Box

[*Socratic: if $x_t$ is I(1) but $y_t$ is I(0), should you difference $x_t$ before including it as a regressor?*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## VAR vs. ARIMAX: When to Use Each

::: {.smaller}

| **Feature** | **VAR** | **ARIMAX** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Direction of causality | Unknown / symmetric | Known (unidirectional) |
| Number of variables | Any $k$ (keep $k\leq 4$) | 1 outcome + regressors |
| Feedback loops | Allowed | Not modeled |
| Interpretability | IRF, FEVD$^\dagger$ | Regression coefficients |
| Forecasting $x_t$ | Joint (endogenous) | Needs external forecast |
| Preferred when... | Macro / finance; symmetric | Clear cause $\to$ effect |

:::

[$^\dagger$FEVD = forecast error variance decomposition: fraction of $y_j$'s forecast variance explained by shocks to $y_i$ at each horizon.]{.neutral .smaller}

::: {.warningbox}
ARIMAX treats $x_t$ as **exogenous**: it assumes $x_t$ does not respond to past $y_t$. If feedback exists, use VAR instead --- otherwise forecasts of $y_t$ will require an external forecast of $x_t$.
:::

---

# Cointegration and Error Correction

Two non-stationary series that share a long-run trend can be modeled together without differencing away their relationship.

---

## Spurious Regression Revisited

**Recall from Lecture 2,** @GrangerNewbold1974: regressing independent random walks yields spuriously high $R^2$. But some I(1) pairs are *genuinely* linked.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Spurious:** independent RWs diverge over time

```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 280 90" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:90px;">
  <line x1="20" y1="75" x2="270" y2="75" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="20" y1="75" x2="20"  y2="5"  stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <polyline
    points="20,65 45,60 70,55 95,57 120,50 145,45 170,47 195,40 220,35 245,38 270,32"
    fill="none" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <polyline
    points="20,65 45,68 70,72 95,76 120,79 145,75 170,80 195,83 220,80 245,85 270,88"
    fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="2.2"/>
</svg>
```

**Cointegrated:** common trend, $y_t - \beta x_t \sim I(0)$

```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 280 90" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:90px;">
  <line x1="20" y1="75" x2="270" y2="75" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="20" y1="75" x2="20"  y2="5"  stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <polyline
    points="20,70 45,64 70,58 95,62 120,55 145,50 170,53 195,46 220,42 245,47 270,40"
    fill="none" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <polyline
    points="20,72 45,67 70,61 95,58 120,58 145,53 170,50 195,49 220,44 245,45 270,42"
    fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="2.2"/>
</svg>
```
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.keybox}
::: {.smaller}
**Engle--Granger test** [@EngleGranger1987; @Hamilton1994]:

1. Regress $y_t$ on $x_t$ (levels)
2. Residuals $\hat{e}_t = y_t - \hat{\beta}x_t$
3. Augmented Dickey--Fuller (ADF) on $\hat{e}_t$: Reject unit root $\Rightarrow$ cointegrated
:::
:::

[*Example: income & consumption --- both I(1), but $c_t - \beta y^d_t \sim I(0)$ (consumption tracks income in the long run).*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

## The Error Correction Model (ECM)

If $y_t$ and $x_t$ are cointegrated with equilibrium $y_t = \beta x_t + u_t$ ($u_t \sim I(0)$):

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Error Correction Model
:::
::: {.smaller}
$$\Delta y_t = \underbrace{\alpha(y_{t-1} - \beta x_{t-1})}_{\text{error correction}} + \underbrace{\gamma_1 \Delta y_{t-1} + \delta_1 \Delta x_{t-1} + \cdots}_{\text{short-run dynamics}} + \varepsilon_t$$

$\alpha < 0$: speed of adjustment; $\gamma_1$: own short-run lag; $\delta_1$: cross short-run lag. If $y_{t-1}$ is *above* equilibrium, the ECM term pushes $\Delta y_t < 0$ (mean-reverting).
:::
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="56%"}
- ECM captures *both* short-run dynamics and long-run equilibrium in one model
- Differenced variables $\Rightarrow$ no spurious regression
- Error correction term $\Rightarrow$ no loss of long-run info
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.warningbox}
::: {.smaller}
**Do not** difference cointegrated series before regression --- this discards the long-run relationship.
:::
:::
:::

::::

[*Example: $\hat{\alpha}=-0.3$, $y_{t-1}-\hat{\beta}x_{t-1}=+2.0$ (10% above equilibrium) $\Rightarrow$ ECM contributes $-0.3\times 2.0 = -0.6$ to $\Delta y_t$ (partial correction).*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# Key Takeaways and Roadmap

VAR, ARIMAX, cointegration, and ECM in one multivariate framework.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.keybox}
::: {.smaller}
1. **Granger causality:** $x_t$ Granger-causes $y_t$ if $x_t$'s past improves forecasts of $y_t$ beyond $y_t$'s own past.

2. **VAR:** symmetric multivariate model estimated equation-by-equation; use IRF to trace shock propagation; keep $k$ small.

3. **ARIMAX:** augment ARIMA with exogenous regressors when causal direction is clear; use SARIMAX in statsmodels.

4. **Cointegration:** two I(1) series with a stable long-run relationship --- model as ECM, not as levels or differences alone.

5. **Model choice:** symmetric dynamics $\to$ VAR; clear cause $\to$ ARIMAX; long-run equilibrium $\to$ ECM.
:::
:::

[*VAR(1) with $a_{12}=a_{21}=0$ collapses to two independent AR(1) models --- the multivariate framework nests the univariate special case.*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## What's Next: Forecast Evaluation

We now have five model families:

- Benchmarks (naïve, seasonal naïve, mean, drift)
- Regression and AR models (Lecture 2)
- Exponential smoothing / ETS (Lecture 3)
- ARIMA / SARIMA (Lecture 4)
- VAR, ARIMAX, ECM (Lecture 5)

**Key question:** how do we rigorously compare these models out-of-sample?

::: {.keybox}
**Lecture 6:** Forecast evaluation --- walk-forward validation, the Diebold--Mariano test [@Diebold1995], and forecast combination.
:::

::: {.neutral .smaller style="text-align:center"}
**Lab 5:** Granger causality tests, VAR fitting and IRF, ARIMAX on RSXFS + consumer sentiment.
:::

---

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
